did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a DeepLabv3+-based convolutional neural network (CNN) for skin image segmentation to extract lesions and skin areas from non-standardized conventional photo images. This AI method was applied to improve computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) performance for classifying skin diseases such as atopic dermatitis, mycosis fungoides, impetigo, and herpesvirus infection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Article' in the header. The abstract describes original experimental work involving the development and training of a DeepLabv3+-based CNN segmentation model, followed by validation using a generated image database to achieve specific sensitivity and specificity metrics for skin disease classification.

title: Convolutional neural network-based skin image segmentation model to improve classification of skin diseases in conventional and non-standardized picture images
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) for skin diseases by developing a DeepLabv3+-based convolutional neural network (CNN) to segment skin and lesion areas from non-standardized conventional photo images. The model was trained to extract images where more than 80% of the area is skin and more than 10% is lesion, addressing the challenge of variable image fields in dermatological practice. The generated CNN-segmented image database was evaluated for classifying atopic dermatitis against malignant diseases and complications like mycosis fungoides, impetigo, and herpesvirus infection. The CAD model achieved approximately 90% sensitivity and specificity using the segmented dataset. The classification accuracy of the CNN-segmented images was found to be almost equal to that of manually cropped images and higher than that of original unsegmented images. The authors conclude that this automatic segmentation method reduces physician annotation burden and enhances CAD performance for multiple skin diseases.

The model achieved approximately 90% sensitivity and specificity.

datasets_used.description: A dataset of non-standardized conventional photo images collected from dermatological practices, containing a mixture of variable fields of view (close-up images focusing on lesions and long-shot images including normal skin and background). The specific source institution or public repository is not explicitly named in the provided text, though the authors are affiliated with Tohoku University.

Manual cropping of images (used as a baseline for comparison against CNN-segmented images)
conclusions: The developed DeepLabv3+-based CNN segmentation model successfully generates a skin disease image dataset suitable for computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) by automatically extracting skin and lesion areas from non-standardized conventional photo images regardless of the image field of view.

The CAD model utilizing the CNN-segmented image database achieved approximately 90% sensitivity and specificity in differentiating atopic dermatitis from malignant diseases and complications (mycosis fungoides, impetigo, and herpesvirus infection).
The classification accuracy using the CNN-segmented image dataset was almost equal to that of manually cropped images and higher than that of the original unsegmented images.
The automatic CNN segmentation method reduces the burden of physician annotation and improves CAD performance for multiple skin diseases.
important_remarks: The study addresses a specific gap in CAD for non-standardized conventional photos, noting that such models typically exhibit lower performance than those using standardized dermoscopic images.
The segmentation criteria are restrictive, requiring images to have >80% skin area and >10% lesion area, which may limit applicability to images with small lesions or significant background.
The dataset consists of non-standardized conventional photo images with variable fields of view and illumination, which are known to significantly influence lesion appearance and diagnosis.
The paper does not report external validation; the model was evaluated on a generated database from the collected images without mention of an independent external test set.

No specific statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) were reported for the comparison between CNN-segmented, manually cropped, and original image datasets, only qualitative comparisons ('almost equal', 'higher').

The study focuses on differentiating atopic dermatitis from malignant diseases and complications (mycosis fungoides, impetigo, herpesvirus infection), limiting generalizability to other skin conditions.

The source of the image data is not explicitly named as a public repository in the provided text, though authors are affiliated with Tohoku University, raising potential concerns about dataset transparency and reproducibility.
